title: Quick: The Kettle's Still Warm
special_element: **The tracks only go in.** In the mud around the post, three or four sets of boot-prints converge on the door from different directions. None lead away. The ground past the threshold is unmarked. Whatever was in this room did not walk out of it.
summary: The door to the Ward Post hangs open a finger's width.
body: The door to the Ward Post hangs open a finger's width. Wood smoke and the sharp bite of scorched oats drift out. Inside: a fire banked to coals, a kettle on its hook still warm when you press your palm to it, and a bowl of porridge at the table — half-eaten, spoon laid neatly across the rim.

Decide one thing before play: whose name is scratched out of the ledger? A party member's name, a name they'll meet later, or a name no living person should know — each lands a different kind of dread. Keep that decision to yourself until it pays off.

Special element: **The tracks only go in.** In the mud around the post, three or four sets of boot-prints converge on the door from different directions. None lead away. The ground past the threshold is unmarked. Whatever was in this room did not walk out of it.
setup: The party reaches Hollow Ward Post to find it left minutes ago. The fire isn't dead. The food isn't cold. A lantern on the wall peg still holds a thread of warmth in its glass. Nothing is overturned, nothing fled-from — whoever was here left carefully, and left fast. There is no monster to fight here. The pressure is the question of what happened, and you let it sit.
escalation: **Opening:** The sensory intro lands. Give the party a beat of silence before they step across the threshold — the warm kettle does the work.

**Complication:** A Perception or Survival check (DC 10) outside spots the inward-only tracks. A second look inside finds one entry in the post ledger scratched out so hard the page is torn through. Decide whose name it was before play.
**Climax:** No creature, no combat. The climax is the realisation — the spoon is still warm, the tracks only go in — and the party looking at each other.
resolution: **Leave:** The unease goes with them. At your discretion, the scratched name is one of theirs.
**Dig deeper:** This scene is the way into the full Hollow Ward Post location. Five minutes is enough to make them want to come back.
**Wait it out:** The fire never quite goes cold. No one comes.
rewards: No loot. The reward is the question.
The torn ledger page, if taken, connects directly to *The Name on the Ledger* character hook.
Advantage on the first Investigation check if the party returns to Hollow Ward Post.

title: Ambush at the Tithe Bridge
special_element: The bandits are the obvious threat — but they are standing on ground that has already decided they haven't paid. The longer anyone lingers, the worse the bridge becomes for *everyone* on it. The party can pay, fight, or talk their way past Vael; the bridge does not distinguish a bandit holding ground from a traveller who hesitates. It counts only what you have left behind.
summary: Category: Encounter · Tier: 1 · Difficulty: medium · Time-to-run: 15-min · Biome: frontier · Mood: unsettling
body: Category: Encounter · Tier: 1 · Difficulty: medium · Time-to-run: 15-min · Biome: frontier · Mood: unsettling

The bridge is one cart's width, no more, and the planks underfoot are cold and damp despite the dry weather. Three figures step out from the far end, blades already loose. The one in front is smiling. From somewhere below the water — slow, rhythmic — something knocks against the underside of the bridge. Once. Twice. As though counting.

Special element: The bandits are the obvious threat — but they are standing on ground that has already decided they haven't paid. The longer anyone lingers, the worse the bridge becomes for *everyone* on it. The party can pay, fight, or talk their way past Vael; the bridge does not distinguish a bandit holding ground from a traveller who hesitates. It counts only what you have left behind.
setup: A bandit trio — Vael and two lackeys — has worked this crossing for two weeks. The location is good: the only bridge for six miles either way, a choke point on the trade road. Their pitch is simple: five silver per head, cross without bleeding.

What no one has told them is that the Tithe Bridge keeps its own toll. It takes something of value from anyone who stops on it with no intent to cross. The bandits have stopped here every day. The bridge has been patient. It is no longer patient.
The knocking is the mechanism: a counting logic built into the wood and stone by whoever raised this span, older than the road it serves. It has tallied what the bandits owe. It has found them short.
escalation: **Opening.** Vael demands five silver per head to cross. The knocking starts — quiet, once every six seconds. The party hears it. The bandits pretend not to.

**Complication.** Round three: the planks under the bandits' feet begin to sweat, a thin cold mist rising off the wood, wrong in a way that's hard to name. A lackey shifts his weight; a plank cracks, holds. Anyone looking over the railing sees dark water and, faintly, a shape that is not a rock.

**Climax.** Round five — or the instant violence begins, whichever comes first — the bridge shudders. Every creature still mid-span makes a DC 12 Dexterity saving throw or falls prone. One plank drops away entirely, opening a gap above the water. The knocking stops. The air goes tight and close. Vael's nerve breaks: he is superstitious, and this is not natural. He will negotiate now, or run.
resolution: **Win — fight or talk.** The bandits flee, surrender, or fall. The bridge settles. Anyone who crossed fully to the far side without pausing feels nothing further. One character who lingered mid-span during the climax hears the knocking one last time — quiet, directly beneath their feet — then silence. They are unharmed. Their coinpurse is one silver lighter. The clasp was never opened.

**Fail — pay and pass.** The party pays Vael and crosses, unhindered. The bandits stay on the bridge to count the coin. The knocking resumes behind the party — louder now. What happens to the bandits is not the party's concern, unless they look back.

**Walk away.** The party refuses and takes the long road. Three days later word reaches them: the crossing is unguarded, the bandits' camp fire was found still lit, and their boots were left in a neat row on the near bank.
scaling: **Tier 1 / medium (as written).** Two Bandits, one Bandit Captain (Vael). The shudder creates chaos and limits the battlefield; it deals no damage.
**Easier.** Two lackeys only, no captain. The bridge only knocks — never shudders.

**Harder.** Four lackeys. The shudder deals 1d6 cold damage on a failed save and leaves the gap as difficult terrain. Vael knows exactly what the bridge is and is using it — he expects the party to pay *him* so the bridge won't collect *from* them. He is not bluffing.
rewards: Five silver per bandit on their persons, plus Vael's coinpurse: 2d6+4 sp and a folded note listing a toll schedule in two different hands.
The one silver taken by the bridge cannot be recovered — the coinpurse is empty, clasp still fastened.

If the party examines the bridge after: a DC 15 History or Arcana check reveals it is older than the road it serves and was not built for travellers. A DM running **The Tithe Bridge** (puzzles) may treat this as the same structure seen from the crossing side; the `related` field handles that link.

title: The Drowned Vault
special_element: **The vault was designed to punish a single thief. It was not designed for a party.** Taking one item triggers the flood. Taking five triggers the same flood. The only winning move is to gather everything worth carrying and cross the threshold simultaneously — all in agreement, all at once — or leave empty-handed. A party that doesn't understand this until someone pockets a coin and turns for the door is a party with four rounds to decide what matters and who carries it. There is no hidden drain. No reset lever. No second chance to read the room.
summary: Category: Encounter · Tier: 2 · Difficulty: hard · Time-to-run: 1-hour · Biome: underwater · Mood: grim
body: Category: Encounter · Tier: 2 · Difficulty: hard · Time-to-run: 1-hour · Biome: underwater · Mood: grim

The door is iron, chest-height, set into a limestone wall furred with black weed — and it is standing open. Beyond it, the water simply stops. A pocket of stale, cold air holds inside, lit by pale mats of growth that glow faintly from the ceiling. The vault is dry. It smells of copper and old wood and something fainter underneath: tallow, as though candles burned here once and went out long ago.

Special element: **The vault was designed to punish a single thief. It was not designed for a party.** Taking one item triggers the flood. Taking five triggers the same flood. The only winning move is to gather everything worth carrying and cross the threshold simultaneously — all in agreement, all at once — or leave empty-handed. A party that doesn't understand this until someone pockets a coin and turns for the door is a party with four rounds to decide what matters and who carries it. There is no hidden drain. No reset lever. No second chance to read the room.
setup: A merchant guild's strongroom, lowered into the harbour bed by design three hundred years ago — the theory being that only a fool would rob a vault you had to drown to reach. The theory held. The vault was last opened during a war no one in the region now remembers, its contents sealed inside when the final keyholder chose not to resurface.

The ward that keeps the water at the threshold is still active. It is not a trap — it was an engineering solution. A binding on the door frame holds the air pocket so long as the vault is in balance. That balance has exactly one condition: **nothing may cross the threshold outward**.

The moment any item — or a creature carrying one — exits the vault, the ward reads the chamber as compromised and collapses. Water floods in at a rate that gives the party roughly four rounds before the chamber is fully submerged. The loot is real. The exit is the same door the water comes through.
escalation: **Opening.** The party enters. The air is breathable and, for now, unlimited — do not make it a resource unless they linger past all reason. Three areas: broken crates of water-ruined coin (mundane, worthless); a locked iron chest (the real loot); a sealed alcove holding a figure still chained to the wall, its empty eye sockets turned toward the door. Let them explore. Nothing reacts to their presence. The ward appears inert.

The chest requires lockpicking (DC 18 Thieves' Tools) or force. Inside: rolled documents in wax-sealed tubes, a heavy gold signet ring, a linen bag of platinum trade coins, and a sealed black glass bottle with something that moves very slightly inside. The party will want to take things.
**Complication.** The first character to pocket something and step through the door triggers it. Water rushes in. Four rounds. The door is still open.

The chained figure begins pulling at its anchor as the water rises; the eye-bolt is tearing loose from the mortar, and the sound is horrible. It does not free itself unless the DM wants a fight — but the clock and the noise arrive together, and the party has to think through both.

**Climax.** Rounds are tactical and social. Who drops what. Who carries the chest (Strength DC 15 to move it at normal speed). Whether they cut losses or coordinate. A party that acts as one — every character crossing the threshold in the same round, each carrying something — satisfies the ward's original trigger by inversion: it was built for one thief, and one thief leaving is the signal. A group exiting together reads as *entry*, not *theft*, and the ward pauses for one round before recognising the collapse. That is enough for a clean exit. Whether the party figures this out is the encounter.
resolution: **They coordinate (win).** The party surfaces with the full take: documents, ring, coins, bottle. The vault fills behind them. The documents are the real prize — letters of guild debt, seals still legally binding, owed by a shipping concern that is very much still operating and will not be pleased.
**Someone panics (partial).** One character exits early. The flood comes. Those still inside swim for it; most of what's loose makes it out. The chest does not.

**They don't understand the mechanic (hard lesson).** They grab and run individually. Everyone surfaces — the vault is not lethal to tier-2 swimmers — but the chest has settled into the silt, the documents are ruined, and they leave with coins and a ring. The vault is now permanently flooded. The air pocket is gone.

**What each exit leaves behind.** The debt documents, in any win, seed the next problem: one seal is owed by a still-extant house that would prefer its debt stayed at the bottom of the harbour. The chained figure is not a hook for this encounter — it is a question. It was chained in, not chained by guards. Whoever went into the vault at the end stayed by choice.
scaling: **Easier:** extend to six rounds; make the ward's trigger visible — runes on the door frame light as the water rises, giving players a legible clock.
**Harder:** a rival diver entered through a submerged window on the far wall and is also looting. Their exit triggers the ward too, and the party does not control them.

**Combat (optional):** if the DM wants a fight, the chained figure animates on round 2 of flooding. Use **Wight** stats; it is restrained (attacks against it have advantage, it cannot move). Treat it as a distraction from the clock, not the main event.
rewards: Platinum trade coins — 300 gp face value; heavy
Gold signet ring — 150 gp; the seal is the real value, it identifies the guild
Wax-sealed debt documents — no immediate gp value; a letter of debt for 2,000 gp, owed by a living shipping concern
Black glass bottle — sealed, opaque, something moves slightly inside; assign or link as the DM sees fit
